content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:codex/gpt-5.4-xhigh

## Summary

Static review found two low-severity fix-now issues in the new timeout plumbing: the new global `BackendRole -> BackendPolicyRole` helper is semantically inconsistent for `CompletionJudge`, and several config-driven requirements-service test/conformance builders were not updated to carry `EffectiveConfig`. I could not run cargo-based verification in this environment because `cargo` is unavailable.

## Amendments

1. `src/shared/domain.rs:270-277` introduces a reusable `BackendRole::to_policy_role()` helper, but `BackendRole::CompletionJudge` is also what `BackendRole::for_stage(StageId::FinalReview)` returns while `stage_to_policy_role(StageId::FinalReview)` still maps to `BackendPolicyRole::FinalReviewer` (`src/contexts/agent_execution/policy.rs:423-425`). `AgentExecutionService::timeout_for_role()` now delegates through that new helper (`src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:178-183`), so any future caller that reuses it for final-review work will silently pick completer timeouts instead of final-review timeouts. Keep this mapping local to requirements, or make the conversion stage/context-aware instead of exposing a misleading global mapping.
   Rationale: The new shared helper bakes a requirements-specific assumption into a cross-cutting API, creating a silent misconfiguration trap for `CompletionJudge` callers outside the current requirements path.
2. `src/contexts/automation_runtime/daemon_loop.rs:5088-5095` and `src/contexts/conformance_spec/scenarios.rs:159-172,295-304` still construct `RequirementsService` from an `EffectiveConfig` without calling `AgentExecutionService::with_effective_config(...)`. After this branch, those builders no longer match production timeout behavior: requirements invocations created through them ignore configured `backends.<family>.timeout_seconds` / `role_timeouts.*` and fall back to the default timeout. Update these helper builders to attach the same `effective_config` clone as the production builder so daemon/conformance regressions can actually exercise the configured-timeout path.
   Rationale: These helpers are explicitly meant to mirror production wiring. Leaving them on the pre-change construction path makes timeout-related tests and conformance coverage diverge from the shipped behavior.
